## AUBER: AUTOMATED BERT REGULARIZATION

## Anonymous authors

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

How can we effectively regularize BERT? Although BERT proves its effectiveness in various downstream natural language processing tasks, it often overfits when there are only a small number of training instances. A promising direction to regularize BERT is based on pruning its attention heads based on a proxy score for head importance. However, heuristic-based methods are usually suboptimal since they predetermine the order by which attention heads are pruned. In order to overcome such a limitation, we propose AUBER, an effective regularization method that leverages reinforcement learning to automatically prune attention heads from BERT. Instead of depending on heuristics or rule-based policies, AUBER learns a pruning policy that determines which attention heads should or should not be pruned for regularization. Experimental results show that AUBER outperforms existing pruning methods by achieving up to 9.39% better accuracy. In addition, our ablation study empirically demonstrates the effectiveness of our design choices for AUBER.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

How can we effectively regularize BERT (Devlin et al. (2018))? In natural language processing (NLP), fine-tuning a large-scale pre-trained language model has greatly enhanced generalization. In particular, BERT has demonstrated effectiveness through improvements in many downstream NLP tasks such as sentence classification and question answering.

Despite its recent success and wide adoption, BERT on a downstream task is prone to overfitting due to over-parameterization; BERT-base has 110M parameters and BERT-large has 340M parameters. The overfitting worsens when the target downstream task only has a small number of training examples. Devlin et al. (2018) and Phang et al. (2018) show that datasets with 10,000 or less training examples sometimes fail to fine-tune BERT.

To mitigate this critical issue, multiple studies attempt to regularize BERT by pruning parameters or using dropout to decrease its model complexity (Michel et al. (2019); Voita et al. (2019); Lee et al. (2020)). Among these approaches, we regularize BERT by pruning attention heads since pruning yields simple and explainable results and it can be used along with other regularization methods. In order to avoid combinatorial search, whose computational complexity grows exponentially with the number of heads, the existing methods measure the importance of each attention head based on heuristics such as an approximation of sensitivity of BERT to pruning a specific attention head. However, these approaches are based on hand-crafted heuristics that are not guaranteed to be directly related to the model performance, and therefore, would result in a suboptimal performance.

In this paper, we propose AUBER, an effective method for regularizing BERT. AUBER overcomes the limitation of past attempts to prune attention heads from BERT by leveraging reinforcement learning. When pruning attention heads from BERT, our method automates this process by learning policies rather than relying on a predetermined rule-based policy and heuristics. AUBER prunes BERT sequentially in a layer-wise manner. For each layer, AUBER extracts features that are useful for the reinforcement learning agent to determine which attention head to be pruned from the current layer. The final pruning policy found by the reinforcement learning agent is used to prune the corresponding layer. Before AUBER proceeds to process the next layer, BERT is fine-tuned to recapture the information lost due to pruning attention heads. An overview of AUBER transitioning from the second to the third layer of BERT is demonstrated in Figure 1.

Our contributions are summarized as follows:

- Method. We propose AUBER for automatically learning to regularize BERT by reinforcement learning. AUBER is designed to carefully represent the state of BERT and reduce the action search cost by dually-greedy search space pruning.
- Analysis. We theoretically justify our design choice of using L1 norm of the value matrix of each attention head as an element of a state (see Theorem 1).

- Experiments. We perform extensive experiments, and show that AUBER successfully regularizes BERT improving the performance by up to 9.39% and outperforms other head pruning methods. Through ablation study, we empirically show that our design choices for AUBER are effective.

## 2 PRELIMINARY

We describe preliminaries on multi-headed self-attention (Section 2.1), BERT (Section 2.2), and deep Q-learning (Section 2.3).

## 2.1 MULTI-HEADED SELF-ATTENTION

An attention function maps a query vector and a set of key-value vector pairs to an output. We compute the query, key, and value vectors by multiplying the input embeddings Q, K, V ∈ R[N][×][d] with the parameterized matrices W[Q] ∈ R[d][×][n] , W[K] ∈ R[d][×][n] , and W[V] ∈ R[d][×][m] respectively, where N is the number of tokens in the sentence, and n, m, and d are query, value, and embedding dimension respectively. In multi-headed attention, H independently parameterized attention heads are applied in parallel to project the input embeddings into multiple representation subspaces. Each attention head contains parameter matrices Wi[Q] ∈ R[d][×][n] , Wi[K] ∈ R[d][×][n] , and Wi[V] ∈ R[d][×][m] . Output matrices of H independent attention heads are concatenated and projected by a matrix W[O] ∈ R[Hm][×][d] to obtain the final result. This process can be represented as:

A multi-headed self-attention follows the same mapping methods as general multi-headed attention function except that all the query, key, and value embeddings come from the same sequence.

## 2.2 BERT

BERT (Devlin et al. (2018)) is a multi-layer Transformer (Vaswani et al. (2017)) pre-trained on masked language model and next sentence prediction tasks. It is then fine-tuned on specific tasks including language inference and question answering. BERT-base has 12 Transformer layers and each layer has 12 self-attention heads. Despite its success in various NLP tasks, BERT sometimes overfits when the training dataset is small due to over-parameterization. Thus, there has been a growing interest in BERT regularization through various methods such as dropout (Lee et al. (2020)).

Figure 2: Overall of AUBER while pruning layer i. It trains DQN and then uses the trained DQN to make the final decision on which heads to prune. 2.3 DEEP Q-LEARNING

A deep Q network (DQN) is a multi-layer neural network that outputs a vector of action-value pairs for a given state s. It is a function that maps a ds-dimensional state space to a da-dimensional action space. Two important features of the DQN algorithm are target network and experience replay (Mnih et al. (2013)). The target network has the same architecture as that of the policy network, and its parameters are copied every τ steps from the policy network. For experience replay, transition tuples (i.e. (state, action, reward, next state)) are stored in a first-in-first-out memory buffer, and a subset of those tuples, uniformly sampled from the buffer, update the parameters of the policy network.

## 3 PROPOSED METHOD

We propose AUBER, our method for regularizing BERT by automatically learning to prune attention heads from BERT. After presenting the overview of the proposed method in Section 3.1, we describe how we frame the problem of pruning attention heads into a reinforcement learning problem in Section 3.2. Section 3.3 explains how states are represented in AUBER and provides a justification for the process. Section 3.4 describes how AUBER reduces the extremely large search space, and describe the full algorithm.

## 3.1 OVERVIEW

head pruning methods rely on hand-crafted heuristics and hyperparameters, which give sub-optimal results. The goal of AUBER is to automate the pruning process for successful regularization. Designing such regularization method entails the following challenges:
1. Automation. How can we automate the head pruning process for regularization without resorting to sub-optimal heuristics?
2. State representation. When automating the regularization process as a reinforcement learning problem, how can we represent the state of BERT in a way useful for the pruning?
3. Action search space scalability. BERT has many parameters and attention heads in each layer. How can we handle prohibitively large action search space for pruning?

We propose the following main ideas to address the challenges:

3

1. Regularization with reinforcement learning (Section 3.2). We exploit reinforcement learning, specifically DQN, with accuracy enhancement as reward. DQN has shown a superior performance for many tasks, and is a natural choice for model-free and off-policy learning (Sutton & Barto (2018)), which is exactly our setting. Experience replay also allows efficient usage of previous experiences and stable convergence (Mnih et al. (2013)).

2. L1 norm of value matrix as state representation (Section 3.3). We use L1 norm of value matrix of each attention head to represent the state of a layer. When a head is pruned, the corresponding value is set to 0.

3. Dually-greedy search space pruning (Section 3.4). To reduce the search space, we use two greedy methods: 1) we prune layer-by-layer, and an action is performed only within a layer, and 2) in each layer, we prune one attention head at a time to reduce the search space.

- 3.2 AUTOMATED REGULARIZATION WITH REINFORCEMENT LEARNING

AUBER leverages reinforcement learning for efficient search of regularization strategy without relying on heuristics. We exploit DQN among various reinforcement learning frameworks which have shown a superior performance in model-free and off-policy environment. The overall flow is described in Figure 2. In this section, we introduce the detailed setting of our reinforcement learning framework.

Initial state. Each layer of BERT has multiple attention heads, each of which has its own query, key, and value matrices. For layer l of BERT, we derive the initial state sl using L1 norm of the value matrix of each attention head. Further details for this computation is elaborated in Section 3.3.

Action. The action space a of AUBER is discrete. For a BERT model with H attention heads per layer, the number of possible actions is H + 1 (i.e. a ∈{1, 2, . . . , H, H + 1}). When the action a = i ∈{1, 2, . . . , H − 1, H} is chosen, the corresponding i[th] attention head is pruned. The action a = H + 1 signals the DQN agent to quit pruning. To facilitate exploration via off-policy learning, actions are chosen in a decaying-epsilon-greedy manner: the agent chooses a random action with probability ϵ that decays over time or otherwise selects an action based on the current policy, where

ϵ = ϵfinal +[ϵ][initial][ −][ϵ][f][inal] (3) e[s/ϵ][decay]

Here, s is the total number of actions taken by the agent up to the current episode, ϵinitial is the starting value of ϵ (i.e. when s = 0), ϵfinal is the value that ϵ converges to as s →∞, and ϵdecay is a hyperparameter that adjusts the rate of decay of ϵ.

Next State. After the i[th] head is pruned, the value of i[th] index of sl is set to 0. This modified state is provided as the next state to the agent. This mechanism allows the agent to recognize which attention heads have been pruned and decide the next best pruning policy based on past decisions. When the action a = H + 1, the next state is set to the terminating state which ends an episode.

Reward. To evaluate the reward, the training data split into two sets: mini-training set and minidev set. We use the data in mini-training set for fine-tuning and data in mini-dev set for reward evaluation. The reward of AUBER is the change in accuracy

∆acc = current accuracy − prev ~~a~~ ccuracy, (4) where current ~~a~~ ccuracy is the accuracy of the current BERT model evaluated on the mini-dev set, and prev ~~a~~ ccuracy is the accuracy obtained from the previous state or the accuracy of the original BERT model if no attention heads are pruned.

If we set the reward simply as current ~~a~~ ccuracy, DQN cannot capture the differences among reward values if the changes in accuracy are relatively small. Setting the reward as the change in accuracy has the normalization effect, thus stabilizing the training process of the DQN agent. The reward for action a = H + 1 is a hyper-parameter that can be adjusted to encourage or discourage active pruning. In AUBER, it is set to 0 to encourage the DQN agent to prune only when the expected change in accuracy is positive.

Fine-tuning. After the best pruning policy for layer l of BERT is found, the BERT model pruned according to the best pruning policy is fine-tuned with a smaller learning rate. This fine-tuning step is crucial since it adjusts the weights of remaining attention heads to compensate for the information lost due to pruning. Then, the initial state of the layer l + 1 is calculated and provided to the agent. Since frequent fine-tuning may lead to overfitting, we separate the training dataset into two: a mini-

validation dataset and a mini-training dataset. The mini-validation dataset is the dataset on which the pruned BERT model is evaluated on to return a reward. After the optimal pruning policy is determined by using the mini-validation dataset, the mini-training dataset is used to fine-tune the pruned model. When all layers are pruned by AUBER, the final model is fine-tuned with the entire training dataset with early stopping.

## 3.3 STATE REPRESENTATION

The initial state sl of layer l of BERT is computed through the following procedure. We first calculate the L1 norm of the value matrix of each attention head. Then, we standardize the norm values to have a mean µ = 0 and a standard deviation σ = 1. Finally, the softmax function is applied to the norm values to yield sl. The justification of using L1 norm is given by Theorem 1 (proof in Appendix A.1) that L1 norm of the value matrix of a head bounds the L1 norm of its output matrix, which implies the importance of the head in the layer.

## 3.4 DUALLY-GREEDY SEARCH SPACE PRUNING

The total number of attention heads in 12-layer BERT is 144, when 12 attention heads are used. Naively designing actions would lead to 2[144] possible actions which are prohibitively large. Our idea to reduce the search space is dually-greedy pruning: we prune layer-by-layer in a greedy manner (from lower to upper layers), and in each layer we greedily prune 1 attention head at a time.

For each layer l with H attention heads, the agent receives an initial layer embedding sl which encodes useful characteristics (l1 norm of the attention heads) of this layer. Then, the agent outputs the index of an attention head that is expected to increase the training accuracy when removed. After an attention head i is pruned, the value of the i[th] index of sl is set to 0, and it is provided as the next state to the agent. This process is repeated until the action a becomes H + 1. The model pruned up to layer l is fine-tuned on the training dataset, and a new initial layer embedding sl+1 is calculated

Algorithm 1 illustrates the process of AUBER. Algorithm 1 receives a BERT model Bt, which is fine-tuned on a specific task t, and the parameters for BERT and reinforcement learning. The algorithm aims to output the regularized Bt. In line 2, we initialize a policy network P and a replay memory M for layer l. Lines 3-28 train the policy network P for E episodes. For each episode, lines 8-25 choose an action based on the current state vector sl, prune an attention head based on the action, compute the resulting reward and the next state s[∗] l[,][and store the transition tuple into] M . This process is repeated until action = H + 1, which indicates the termination of pruning. In line 27, we optimize P with transition tuples sampled from M . Finally, in lines 29-30, we use the trained policy network to find the optimal pruning policy for layer l, prune Bt according to the policy, and finally fine-tune Bt. After pruning a layer l, we proceed to prune the next layer l + 1 up to layer L.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

We conduct experiments to answer the following questions of AUBER.

- Q1 Accuracy (Section 4.2). Given a BERT model fine-tuned on a specific NLP task, how well does AUBER improve the accuracy of the model?
- Q2 State Representation (Section 4.3). How useful is the L1 norm of the value matrices of attention heads in representing the state of BERT?
- Q3 Order of Processing Layers (Section 4.4). How does the order in which the layers are processed by AUBER affect regularization?

BERT Model. We use the pre-trained bert-base-cased model with 12 layers and 12 attention heads per layer provided by huggingface[5] . We fine-tune this model on each dataset mentioned in Table 1 to obtain the initial model. Initial models for MRPC, CoLA, and WNLI are fine-tuned on the corresponding dataset for 3 epochs, and the initial model for RTE is fine-tuned for 4 epochs. The maximum sequence length is set to 128, and the batch size per GPU is set to 32. The learning rate for fine-tuning initial models for MRPC, CoLA, and WNLI is set to 0.00002, and the learning rate for fine-tuning the initial model for RTE is set to 0.00001.

Reinforcement Learning. We use a 4-layer feedforward neural network for the DQN agent. The dimensions of input, output, and all hidden layers are set to 12, 13, and 512, respectively. LeakyReLU is applied after all layers except for the last one. We train the DQN agent for 150 episodes. For the epsilon greedy strategy for choosing actions, ϵinitial and ϵfinal are set to 1 and 0.05 respectively, and the epsilon decreases exponentially with the decay rate ϵdecay of 256. The replay memory size is set to 5000, and the batch size for training the DQN agent is set to 128. The discount value γ for the DQN agent is set to 1. The learning rate is set to 0.000002 when fine-tuning BERT after processing a layer. Before processing each layer, the training dataset is randomly split into 1 : 2 to yield a mini-training dataset and a mini-validation dataset. When fine-tuning the final model, the patience value of early stopping is set to 20.

## 4.2 ACCURACY

We evaluate the performance of AUBER against competitors. Table 2 shows the results on four GLUE datasets listed in Table 1. Note that AUBER outperforms all of its competitors on regularizing BERT, providing the best accuracy for all the datasets. While most of the competitors fail to improve performance of BERT on the dev dataset of MRPC and CoLA, AUBER improves the performance of BERT by up to 9.39% on those datasets.

4.3 EFFECT OF STATE REPRESENTATION

We validate that the L1 norm of value matrix of each attention head effectively guides AUBER to predict the best action. Table 3 shows the performances of the variants of AUBER.

## 4.3.1 AUBER WITH THE KEY/QUERY MATRICES AS THE STATE VECTOR

Among the query, key, and value matrices of each attention head, we show that the value matrix best represents the current state of BERT. Here we evaluate the performance of AUBER against AUBER-Query and AUBER-Key. AUBER-Query and AUBER-Key use the query and key matrices respectively to obtain the initial state. Note that AUBER, which uses the value matrix to obtain state vectors, outperforms AUBER-Query and AUBER-Key on all four tasks.

## 4.3.2 AUBER WITH L2 NORM OF THE VALUE MATRICES AS THE STATE VECTOR

L1 norm of the value matrices is used to compute the state vector based on the theoretical derivation. In this ablation study, we experimentally show that the L1 norm of the value matrices is appropriate for state vector. We set a new variant AUBER-L2 which leverages L2 norm of the value matrices to compute the initial state vector instead of L1 norm. The performance of AUBER is far more superior than AUBER-L2 in most cases bolstering that L1 norm of the value matrices effectively represents the state of BERT.

## 4.4 EFFECT OF ORDER OF PROCESSING LAYERS

mance. We evaluate the performance of AUBER against AUBER-Reverse which processes BERT layers in the opposite direction (i.e. starting from the 12[th] layer) to what AUBER does. As shown in Table 3, AUBER provides a better performance than AUBER-Reverse in 3 out of 4 cases. This shows that pruning lower layers first and then move to upper layers by AUBER is effective. A possible explanation is that it is easier to train task-specific parameters (those in upper layers) after learning general parameters (those in lower layers), rather than that in the reverse order.

## 5 RELATED WORK

BERT Regularization. To prevent overfitting of BERT on downstream NLP tasks, various regularization techniques have been proposed. Variants of dropout improves the stability of fine-tuning large pre-trained language models even when presented with a small number of training examples (Lee et al. (2020); Fan et al. (2020)). Using slanted triangular learning rate schedule and discriminative fine-tuning has been proven to effectively prevent overfitting (Howard & Ruder (2018)). Jiang et al. (2019) propose SMART, which regularizes the model by smoothing it and preventing aggressive update while Liu et al. (2019) train BERT model with multi-task learning algorithm to remedy overfitting. Introducing adversarial training to enhance the generalization of BERT has been tackled in Zhu et al. (2020). Pre-training tasks have also been changed for a better regularization. Xu et al. (2020) modify the pre-training task, next sentence prediction, to additionally predict previous sentence so that it can capture more correlation. Our method has definite advantages for it can be used along with any of the aforementioned methods.

BERT Pruning. A number of studies have analyzed the effectiveness of pruning parameters in BERT. Gordon et al. (2020) experimentally prove overparameterization of BERT by showing that pruning 30 − 40% of parameters does not affect model performance. Chen et al. (2020) find out the best parameter pruning strategy from the viewpoint of the lottery ticket hypothesis while Guo et al. (2019) deploy reweighted L1 regularization with proximal algorithm. However, these methods primarily aim to compress BERT model not to regularize it, and, therefore, no specific method to enhance the model performance has been proposed.

Thanks to the unique structure of BERT that consists of multi-headed attention, not only parameter pruning but also structured attention head pruning has been addressed (Voita et al. (2019); Michel et al. (2019); Kovaleva et al.; McCarley (2019)). Michel et al. (2019); Kovaleva et al.; Prasanna et al. (2020) evaluate the role and importance of each attention head by measuring heuristics: the average of its maximum attention weight, where average is taken over tokens in a set of sentences used for evaluation, or the expected sensitivity of the model to attention head pruning. Their results show that a large percentage of attention heads with low importance scores can be pruned without significantly impacting performance. The approach to set L0 regularization term to minimize both the training loss and the number of used attention heads has been also presented (Voita et al. (2019); McCarley (2019)). However, they usually yield suboptimal results since they predetermine the order in which the attention heads are pruned by using heuristics.

Automation of Neural Network Pruning To automate the process of Convolutional Neural Network pruning, Lin et al.; He & Han (2018) have leveraged reinforcement learning to determine the best pruning strategy for each layer. Important features that characterize a layer are provided to a reinforcement learning agent to determine how much of the current layer should be pruned. To the best of our knowledge, AUBER is the first attempt to use reinforcement learning to prune attention heads from Transformer-based models such as BERT.

## 6 CONCLUSION

We propose AUBER, an effective method to regularize BERT by automatically pruning attention heads. Instead of depending on heuristics or rule-based policies, AUBER leverages reinforcement learning to learn a pruning policy that determines which attention heads should be pruned for better regularization. Experimental results demonstrate that AUBER effectively regularizes BERT, increasing the performance of the original model on the dev dataset by up to 9.39%. In addition, we experimentally demonstrate the effectiveness of our design choices for AUBER.

coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.label: Coarse (qwen3-235b-a22b-2507)
coarse__qwen3-235b-a22b-2507.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes AUBER, a reinforcement learning-based method to automate BERT regularization via attention head pruning. It makes a clear contribution by replacing heuristic-based pruning with a learned policy, and reports strong empirical gains—up to 9.39% improvement—on low-data GLUE tasks. The integration of DQN with layer-wise pruning and fine-tuning is conceptually sound, and ablation studies support key design choices. However, several macro-level issues limit confidence in the robustness, reproducibility, and generalizability of the approach.

Overview issues:

- Reward definition incentivizes minimal pruning and may collapse to trivial policies: The reward is defined as the change in accuracy on a mini-dev set (Equation 4), with a terminal reward of 0 for stopping. This creates a risk that the agent learns to prune nothing to avoid negative rewards, especially given the stochasticity in fine-tuning and evaluation. The paper does not report how often the agent chooses to stop early or how many heads are actually pruned per layer. Without a negative penalty for not pruning, and with no exploration bonus, the policy may degenerate into inaction. This is especially concerning because the claimed accuracy gains could stem from the fine-tuning step rather than the pruning decision. The authors should either introduce a cost for model complexity or demonstrate that non-trivial pruning occurs consistently across runs.

- State representation lacks dynamic context and may not support generalization: The state is based solely on the L1 norm of value matrices, standardized and softmaxed (Section 3.3). While Theorem 1 provides a theoretical bound, it does not establish that this scalar per head captures sufficient information for effective control. The state ignores query and key dynamics, inter-head interactions, and task-specific gradients. This risks making the agent myopic: it cannot distinguish between heads that are redundant versus those that are task-critical but low-magnitude. The ablation in Table 3 compares alternative norms and matrices but does not test richer representations. Without evidence that this state supports cross-task generalization, the method may overfit to the specific datasets used.

- Layer-wise sequential pruning breaks credit assignment and ignores global structure: AUBER prunes layers sequentially from bottom to top, fine-tuning after each. This decouples the pruning decisions across layers, preventing the agent from learning long-range dependencies—e.g., that pruning a lower-layer head may necessitate preserving a specific upper-layer head. The dually-greedy approach reduces search space but assumes layer independence, which contradicts known findings about hierarchical attention flow in BERT. The ablation on reverse order (AUBER-Reverse) is suggestive but insufficient: it does not test whether joint layer pruning could yield better policies. This design choice may limit the method’s ceiling performance and introduces a strong architectural bias not justified by theory or evidence.

- Evaluation lacks statistical rigor and fails to control for fine-tuning variance: Table 2 reports mean ± std over runs, but it is unclear how many independent trials were conducted or whether the same random seed affects both pruning and fine-tuning. The reward depends on a mini-dev set, which introduces high variance when datasets are small (e.g., WNLI has only 71 dev examples). The paper does not report p-values or confidence intervals to support claims of superiority. Moreover, the fine-tuning step after pruning—critical to performance—varies across methods, yet no ablation isolates its contribution. Without controlling for fine-tuning stochasticity, the observed gains may not be attributable to the pruning policy itself.

- Action space and exploration strategy may hinder convergence: The action space allows pruning one head at a time or stopping (Section 3.2), with a decaying epsilon-greedy policy. However, the agent must learn a sequential decision process with delayed rewards: the benefit of pruning a head may only manifest after downstream fine-tuning and evaluation. With no reward shaping or intermediate incentives, and with exploration decaying over episodes, the agent may fail to discover beneficial long-term strategies. The lack of curriculum or warm-start from heuristic policies increases sample inefficiency. This raises concerns about reproducibility, especially given the small replay buffer (5000) and high-dimensional state transitions.

- Scalability and computational cost are unaddressed despite claims of automation: The method trains a DQN for 150 episodes per layer, with full fine-tuning after each pruning decision. For 12 layers, this results in hundreds of fine-tuning runs per task. The paper does not report wall-clock time or compute cost, nor compare it to manual or heuristic methods. Given that BERT fine-tuning is already expensive, this approach may be prohibitively costly for practical use. The automation benefit is undermined if the search cost exceeds the savings from reduced model size. A cost-benefit analysis is needed to justify the method’s viability beyond small-scale experiments.

- Generalization claims are unsupported by narrow task selection: Experiments are limited to four GLUE tasks with small training sets. While these are appropriate for studying overfitting, they do not demonstrate whether AUBER generalizes to larger datasets, different architectures (e.g., RoBERTa, DeBERTa), or non-classification tasks like QA or NLI with longer sequences. The method is evaluated only on BERT-base, and no ablation tests transfer to other models. Without broader evaluation, the claim of being a general BERT regularization method is premature. At minimum, the authors should test on one larger-dataset task to assess robustness.

- No evidence that pruning—not fine-tuning—drives performance gains: The paper attributes accuracy improvements to AUBER's pruning policy, but the experimental design confounds pruning with fine-tuning. Every pruning decision is followed by fine-tuning, and the final model undergoes full fine-tuning with early stopping. This makes it impossible to isolate whether gains come from the specific heads pruned or simply from the regularization effect of fine-tuning a smaller model. Readers will wonder if any structured pruning (or even random pruning) followed by aggressive fine-tuning would yield similar benefits. To disentangle this, the authors should add an ablation where the final model is evaluated immediately after pruning, without any post-pruning fine-tuning, or compare against a baseline that applies the same fine-tuning schedule to randomly pruned models. Without this, the core claim—that AUBER learns an effective pruning policy—is not fully supported.

- Missing computation of actual search cost and comparison to alternatives: The paper claims AUBER reduces action search cost via dually-greedy pruning, but it never quantifies the actual computational cost of the full pipeline. For each layer, the method runs 150 DQN episodes, each involving forward passes, pruning, evaluation on a mini-dev set, and replay updates. This is computationally intensive compared to one-shot heuristic methods like Michel et al. (2019). A direct comparison of wall-clock time or GPU-hours between AUBER and its competitors is necessary to assess whether the automation is practical. At minimum, the authors should report the total number of fine-tuning steps or forward-backward passes required per task and compare it to the cost of standard fine-tuning or one-shot pruning. Without this, the scalability claims are unsubstantiated.

- No test of policy transfer across tasks or models: The current experiments train a separate DQN agent for each task and dataset, which limits practical utility. A key question is whether the learned pruning policy generalizes—e.g., can a policy trained on CoLA be applied to RTE with similar gains? This would strengthen the claim of automation and reduce the per-task cost. The paper should test zero-shot transfer: train AUBER on one task (e.g., MRPC), then apply the final pruning policy to another without retraining. Alternatively, train a single agent across multiple tasks in a multi-task setup. Without any transfer evaluation, the method appears to be a per-task tuning procedure rather than a general regularization tool.

- Lack of head selection transparency and interpretability: The paper does not report which attention heads AUBER prunes—e.g., their layer distribution, position (query, key, value), or known functional roles (as identified in prior work like Michel et al. 2019). This makes it difficult to assess whether the policy aligns with known patterns (e.g., pruning more in lower layers, preserving syntactic heads). A simple histogram showing the number of pruned heads per layer across tasks would help. Even better, compute the overlap between heads pruned by AUBER and those identified as redundant by existing heuristics. Without such analysis, the policy remains a black box, and readers cannot judge whether the method discovers meaningful structure.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Re-evaluate the reward design to ensure non-trivial pruning policies are learned; report average number of heads pruned and include ablation on reward variants.

- Provide evidence that the state representation captures meaningful layer dynamics—e.g., through attention pattern analysis or ablation with richer features.

- Conduct additional experiments to isolate the contribution of fine-tuning from pruning decisions, including control baselines with identical fine-tuning schedules.

- Expand evaluation to at least two additional tasks with larger datasets and report computational cost and training time.

- Clarify reproducibility by reporting number of random seeds, statistical significance of results, and sensitivity to hyperparameters like mini-dev split ratio.

comments.title: Incorrect constant in Theorem 1 bound
comments.quote: Then,  $\| O_i \|_1 \leq C \| W_i^V \|_1$  where the constant  $C = N \| V \|_1$ .
comments.explanation: The claimed bound $ \| O_i \|_1 \leq C \| W_i^V \|_1 $ with $ C = N \| V \|_1 $ fails dimensional consistency. The output $ O_i \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times m} $ has L1 norm scaling with $ N m $, while $ C \| W_i^V \|_1 $ scales with $ N^2 d^2 m $, introducing a mismatch by $ N d^2 $. A correct bound should involve entrywise or operator norms—e.g., $ \| O_i \|_1 \leq N \cdot \| V \|_\infty \cdot \| W_i^V \|_1 $—to maintain dimensional coherence. The current form cannot hold uniformly across input sizes and undermines the theoretical justification for using $ \| W_i^V \|_1 $ in state representation.

comments.title: State update mismatches implementation reality
comments.quote: After an attention head  $i$  is pruned, the value of the  $i^{th}$  index of  $s_l$  is set to 0, and it is provided as the next state to the agent.
comments.explanation: Zeroing the $i^{th}$ entry of $s_l$ assumes the model retains the original indexing after pruning, but in standard implementations (e.g., Hugging Face), pruning a head reshapes weight matrices and reindexes remaining heads. This creates a mismatch: the agent may attempt to prune a head that no longer exists or has shifted index. The feedback loop must reflect actual model structure—either by reducing state dimension or using a dynamic mask. Otherwise, the agent operates on a logically inconsistent state, risking invalid actions and unstable learning.

comments.title: Reward definition creates degenerate policy incentive
comments.quote: The reward for action $a=H+1$ is a hyper-parameter that can be adjusted to encourage or discourage active pruning. In AUBER, it is set to $0$ to encourage the DQN agent to prune only when the expected change in accuracy is positive.
comments.explanation: Setting the terminal reward to 0 while defining pruning rewards as $\Delta \text{acc}$ creates a structural bias against pruning: any pruning action with non-positive reward is worse than stopping immediately. Since accuracy changes on small mini-dev sets are noisy and often near zero, the agent is incentivized to stop at the first step. This risks a degenerate 'do nothing' policy. To encourage exploration of multi-step pruning sequences, the terminal reward should be negative or a per-step cost introduced to break symmetry between inaction and pruning.

comments.title: Misattribution of output norm bound to head importance
comments.quote: The justification of using L1 norm is given by Theorem 1 (proof in Appendix A.1) that L1 norm of the value matrix of a head bounds the L1 norm of its output matrix, which implies the importance of the head in the layer.
comments.explanation: Theorem 1 references $ \| W_i^V \|_1 $, the parameter norm, but the state uses $ \| V W_i^V \|_1 $, the activation norm. These are distinct: the former is input-independent, the latter depends on $ V $. A head with small $ \| W_i^V \|_1 $ may still have large output if $ V $ is large, and vice versa. The justification thus conflates parameter magnitude with activation magnitude, undermining the link between state representation and head importance. The theorem or its interpretation must be revised to align with the actual state computation.

comments.title: Standardization step not preserved after softmax
comments.quote: Then, we standardize the norm values to have a mean  $\mu = 0$  and a standard deviation  $\sigma = 1$ . Finally, the softmax function is applied to the norm values to yield  $s_l$ .
comments.explanation: Standardization (mean 0, std 1) is destroyed by the subsequent softmax, which is nonlinear and monotonic but not affine-invariant. For example, standardized values $[-2, 0, 2]$ become $[e^{-2}, 1, e^2]/Z$, which is neither zero-mean nor unit-variance. The resulting $s_l$ loses the intended normalization, and softmax compresses values into a probability simplex, making the state sensitive to outliers. Either remove standardization or apply it after softmax to maintain statistical properties.

comments.title: Discount factor γ = 1 invalidates convergence in episodic RL
comments.quote: The discount value  $\gamma$  for the DQN agent is set to 1.
comments.explanation: Setting $\gamma = 1$ in an episodic task with variable-length episodes violates DQN convergence conditions. Since episodes end only when the agent chooses to stop, and no maximum length is imposed, cumulative rewards could diverge. Even with bounded episodes, $\gamma = 1$ increases variance in Q-value estimation. Standard practice uses $\gamma < 1$ (e.g., 0.99) to ensure finite expected returns and stable learning. The authors must justify $\gamma = 1$ or correct it to avoid theoretical instability.

comments.title: Mismatch in fine-tuning learning rate across stages
comments.quote: The learning rate is set to 0.000002 when fine-tuning BERT after processing a layer.
comments.explanation: The learning rate of 2e-6 for post-layer fine-tuning is an order of magnitude smaller than the initial fine-tuning rates (2e-5 for most tasks). Such a low rate may prevent effective adaptation after pruning, as structural changes require significant parameter updates. This risks freezing the network and undermining the purpose of interleaved fine-tuning. The agent may receive misleading reward signals because the model fails to recover. The learning rate should match the initial fine-tuning rate unless justified otherwise.

comments.title: Incorrect mini-dev split ratio undermines reward stability
comments.quote: Before processing each layer, the training dataset is randomly split into  $1:2$  to yield a mini-training dataset and a mini-validation dataset.
comments.explanation: A 1:2 split allocates one-third of training data to validation—over 200 examples for WNLI—which severely limits the effective training size for fine-tuning. Given that the reward depends on this mini-dev set, such a large split inflates variance and risks unstable pruning decisions. The ratio is likely reversed: it should be 2:1 (mini-train:mini-dev) to allocate the majority to training. Otherwise, the reward signal becomes unreliable due to small effective training batches.

comments.title: Action space definition contradicts implementation
comments.quote: When the action $a=i\in\{1,2,\ldots,H-1,H\}$ is chosen, the corresponding $i^{th}$ attention head is pruned. The action $a=H+1$ signals the DQN agent to quit pruning.
comments.explanation: The action space allows re-pruning of already-removed heads because the indices remain fixed after pruning. The paper does not specify masking of invalid actions, which is standard in RL for combinatorial spaces. Without masking, the agent wastes exploration on illegal moves, reducing sample efficiency and potentially destabilizing learning. The method should either mask pruned heads or dynamically reindex the action space to reflect remaining heads.

comments.title: State vector dimension mismatch after softmax
comments.quote: Finally, the softmax function is applied to the norm values to yield  $s_l$ .
comments.explanation: The state $s_l$ is derived from $H$ L1 norms and thus has dimension $H$, but the action space has $H+1$ options (prune one of $H$ heads or stop). When the algorithm accesses $s_l[\text{action}]$ for action $H+1$, it references an undefined index. The state must support the full action space—e.g., by adding a scalar feature like fraction of unpruned heads or a bias term for the stop action—otherwise the policy network cannot properly evaluate the stop decision.

comments.title: Overstated action space size
comments.quote: Naively designing actions would lead to  $2^{144}$  possible actions which are prohibitively large.
comments.explanation: The claim of $2^{144}$ configurations assumes arbitrary subsets of heads can be pruned, but valid configurations must preserve per-layer multi-head structure—i.e., each layer retains an integer number of heads between 0 and 12. This reduces the space to $13^{12} \approx 2.3\times10^{13}$, still large but far smaller than $2^{144} \approx 2.2\times10^{43}$. The exaggeration weakens the motivation for search space pruning. The paper should clarify whether it considers only structurally valid prunings and adjust the justification accordingly.

comments.title: Incorrect symbol for L1 norm in state representation
comments.quote: Q2 State Representation (Section 4.3). How useful is the  $LI$  norm of the value matrices of attention heads in representing the state of BERT?
comments.explanation: The symbol 'LI' is a typographical error for 'L1'. The intended norm is clearly the L1 norm, as established in Section 3.3 and Theorem 1. Using 'LI' introduces confusion and appears unprofessional. This error persists in the section header and question framing, suggesting it was not caught in proofreading. The text should be corrected to use 'L1' consistently.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes AUBER, an RL-based method for automatically pruning BERT attention heads to regularize the model. While the approach is novel and shows promising empirical results, there are significant concerns about the computational efficiency of the RL search, the adequacy of the state representation, and the generalizability of the findings. The paper lacks thorough analysis of computational trade-offs and comparison with more recent dynamic compression methods.

AUBER presents an interesting application of reinforcement learning to automate BERT regularization through attention head pruning. The method shows consistent improvements over heuristic-based baselines on small GLUE datasets, and the ablation studies provide some validation of design choices. The theoretical justification for using L1 norm of value matrices is a nice touch. However, the paper falls short on analyzing the computational costs of the RL training process and demonstrating the transferability of learned pruning policies across tasks.

Overview issues:

- Computational Cost of RL Training Not Justified: The paper claims AUBER overcomes limitations of heuristic methods but provides no analysis of whether the computational cost of training 12 separate DQN agents (one per layer) is justified. Each agent requires 150 episodes with multiple fine-tuning steps per episode. This represents a substantial computational overhead compared to simple heuristic pruning methods. The paper should include: (1) runtime comparisons showing total training time for AUBER vs baselines, (2) analysis of whether the accuracy gains (up to 9.39%) justify the additional compute cost, and (3) discussion of scenarios where this trade-off would or would not be worthwhile. Without this analysis, readers cannot assess the practical utility of the approach.

- State Representation May Be Insufficient for Complex Decisions: The paper uses only L1 norm of value matrices as the state representation, justified by Theorem 1 which shows this bounds the output norm. However, this single scalar per head appears insufficient to capture the complex interactions between heads that affect pruning decisions. The state does not include: (1) information about attention patterns or which tokens heads focus on, (2) gradients or sensitivity information that other methods use, or (3) relationships between heads (e.g., redundancy). While the ablation shows L1 outperforms L2 and using key/query matrices, this doesn't prove the representation is adequate - only that it's better than these specific alternatives. The paper should analyze whether more informative state representations could further improve performance.

- Task-Specific Pruning Limits Practical Utility: AUBER learns task-specific pruning policies, requiring the expensive RL training process for each new task. The paper does not address whether pruned heads transfer across tasks or whether a single pruning policy could work for multiple tasks. This is a significant limitation compared to methods that find generally unimportant heads. The paper should: (1) test whether heads pruned for one task remain unimportant for others, (2) analyze the overlap between pruned heads across different tasks, and (3) discuss the feasibility of learning task-agnostic pruning policies. Without this, the method's applicability is limited to scenarios where one can afford task-specific RL training.

- Missing Comparison with Dynamic Compression Methods: The paper only compares with static pruning methods that remove heads permanently. Recent work like DynaBERT (mentioned in literature context) trains models that can dynamically adjust size at inference time. This is a significant omission since dynamic methods offer flexibility that static pruning cannot. The paper should compare against at least one dynamic compression method to show when AUBER's static pruning is preferable. Additionally, the comparison with baselines may be unfair - all methods are forced to prune exactly P heads, but different methods might prefer different pruning levels for optimal performance.

- Insufficient Analysis of Fine-Tuning Stability: AUBER involves multiple fine-tuning stages: after each layer's pruning decision, and a final fine-tuning on all data. The paper splits data into mini-training/mini-validation sets for each layer, which introduces variability. However, there's no analysis of: (1) sensitivity to the random data splits, (2) how performance varies with different fine-tuning hyperparameters, or (3) whether the multiple fine-tuning stages could lead to overfitting on small datasets. This is particularly important since the paper targets small datasets where overfitting is already a concern. The paper should include stability analysis across different random seeds and data splits.

- No demonstration of non-vacuous theoretical result: Theorem 1 provides a theoretical justification for using L1 norm of value matrices, but the paper never demonstrates this result has practical bite. The theorem shows ||O_i||_1 ≤ C ||W_i^V||_1, but we don't see a concrete example where this bound is tight or where heads with small L1 norms are indeed less important. The paper should include a worked example on a small BERT model showing: (1) compute the L1 norms for each head, (2) verify the bound holds numerically, and (3) demonstrate that pruning heads with the smallest norms actually improves performance in at least one case. Without this, Theorem 1 remains an abstract justification rather than a practical tool.

- Missing computational feasibility analysis: The paper claims AUBER reduces search space through dually-greedy pruning but provides no analysis of whether the method is computationally feasible in practice. Training 12 separate DQN agents with 150 episodes each, plus multiple fine-tuning steps, represents substantial overhead. The paper needs to include: (1) total wall-clock time comparisons with baseline methods, (2) GPU memory requirements during RL training, and (3) analysis of how computational cost scales with model size (e.g., BERT-large). Readers cannot assess if the 9.39% accuracy gain justifies what could be days of additional training time.

- No guidance on hyperparameter selection: AUBER introduces multiple new hyperparameters (epsilon decay rate, replay memory size, DQN architecture, number of episodes) but provides no guidance on setting them. The paper reports specific values used but doesn't explain: (1) sensitivity analysis showing how performance varies with these choices, (2) whether the same hyperparameters work across different tasks or model sizes, or (3) practical rules of thumb for practitioners. Without this guidance, reproducing the results or applying AUBER to new tasks becomes a matter of trial and error, undermining the method's practical utility.

- Unexplored task transferability of pruning policies: The paper demonstrates AUBER learns task-specific pruning policies but never investigates whether these policies transfer across tasks. This is a critical gap since training a new DQN for each task is expensive. The paper should include: (1) an experiment applying the pruning policy learned on MRPC to other GLUE tasks, (2) analysis of overlap between pruned heads across different tasks, and (3) discussion of when a task-agnostic pruning policy might be preferable. Without this, readers cannot determine if AUBER is only suitable for single-task scenarios or if the learned patterns have broader applicability.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Add comprehensive computational efficiency analysis comparing AUBER's training time and resource requirements against baseline methods

- Investigate transferability of pruning policies across tasks and analyze overlap in pruned heads between different GLUE tasks

- Compare against dynamic compression methods (e.g., DynaBERT) to clarify when static vs dynamic pruning is preferable

- Analyze stability of the multi-stage fine-tuning process across different random seeds and data splits

- Explore richer state representations that capture more information about attention head interactions and patterns

comments.title: Epsilon decay formula implements increasing epsilon
comments.quote: $\epsilon=\epsilon_{final}+\frac{\epsilon_{initial}-\epsilon_{final}}{e^{s/\epsilon_{decay}}}$ (3)
comments.explanation: The epsilon decay formula has the exponential in the denominator, which means epsilon increases with s rather than decays. As s increases, e^{s/\epsilon_{decay}} grows, making the fraction smaller, so epsilon approaches epsilon_final from above. This is the opposite of the intended decaying behavior. The correct formula should have epsilon in the numerator: $\epsilon=\epsilon_{final}+(\epsilon_{initial}-\epsilon_{final}) \cdot e^{-s/\epsilon_{decay}}$.

comments.title: State update breaks probability distribution
comments.quote:
$s_{l}^{*} \gets \text{copy}(s_{l})$
$s_{l}^{*}[\text{action}] \gets 0$
comments.explanation: The state update mechanism violates the probability distribution property. Since s_l is defined as softmax output (summing to 1), setting s_l*[action] = 0 without renormalization means the updated state no longer sums to 1. This breaks the Markov property assumption that states remain in the valid state space. Replace the two lines with "$s_{l}^{*} \gets \text{copy}(s_{l})$; $s_{l}^{*}[\text{action}] \gets 0$; $s_{l}^{*} \gets \text{softmax}(s_{l}^{*})$" to maintain the probability distribution after each pruning action.

comments.title: Standardization before softmax creates division by zero
comments.quote: We first calculate the L1 norm of the value matrix of each attention head. Then, we standardize the norm values to have a mean  $\mu = 0$  and a standard deviation  $\sigma = 1$ . Finally, the softmax function is applied to the norm values to yield  $s_l$ .
comments.explanation: The standardization step before softmax has a critical edge case. When all L1 norms are identical (possible in early training stages or symmetric layers), the standard deviation σ = 0, making standardization undefined due to division by zero. Add "If σ = 0, set all standardized values to 0 (or equal values) before applying softmax" after the standardization step to handle the degenerate case where all norms are equal.

comments.title: Theorem 1 bound missing attention weights
comments.quote: Theorem 1. For a layer with  $H$  heads, let  $N$  be the number of tokens in the sentence and  $m$ ,  $n$ , and  $d$  be the value, query, and embedding dimension respectively. Let  $Q, K, V \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$  be the input query, key, and value matrices, and  $W_i^Q$ ,  $W_i^K$ , and  $W_i^V$  be the weight parameters of the  $i^{th}$  head such that  $W_i^Q, W_i^K \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times n}$  and  $W_i^V \in \mathbb{R}^{d \times m}$ . Let  $O_i$  be the output of the  $i^{th}$  head. Then,  $||O_i||_1 \leq C ||W_i^V||_1$  where the constant  $C = N \| V \|_1$
comments.explanation: Theorem 1's bound omits the attention weights which are crucial for the actual output. The head output is O_i = softmax(QW_i^Q(KW_i^K)^T/√d) VW_i^V, not just VW_i^V. The attention matrix A_i = softmax(QW_i^Q(KW_i^K)^T/√d) has rows summing to 1, so ||A_i||_1 = N. Including this gives ||O_i||_1 ≤ ||A_i||_1 ||V||_1 ||W_i^V||_1 = N ||V||_1 ||W_i^V||_1. The theorem should state "Then, ||O_i||_1 ≤ N ||V||_1 ||W_i^V||_1" and explain that the N factor comes from the attention weights.

comments.title: Inconsistent fine-tuning epochs across datasets
comments.quote: Initial models for MRPC, CoLA, and WNLI are fine-tuned on the corresponding dataset for 3 epochs, and the initial model for RTE is fine-tuned for 4 epochs.
comments.explanation: The paper provides no justification for why RTE receives 4 epochs while the other three datasets receive 3 epochs. Since the paper claims to test on small datasets where BERT often fails, and all four datasets have under 10,000 training instances, this differential treatment needs explanation. The different number of epochs could affect the initial model quality and subsequent pruning decisions, potentially biasing the comparison.

comments.title: Missing learning rate justification for RTE
comments.quote: The learning rate for fine-tuning initial models for MRPC, CoLA, and WNLI is set to 0.00002, and the learning rate for fine-tuning the initial model for RTE is set to 0.00001.
comments.explanation: Similar to the epoch inconsistency, RTE uses a different learning rate (0.00001) compared to the other three datasets (0.00002). The paper provides no explanation for this discrepancy. Since learning rate significantly impacts BERT fine-tuning performance, this difference could confound the results.

comments.title: Contradiction between text and Table 3 results
comments.quote: Note that AUBER outperforms all of its competitors on regularizing BERT, providing the best accuracy for all the datasets.
comments.explanation: The text claims AUBER provides the best accuracy for all datasets, but Table 3 shows AUBER-Reverse achieves 86.52% accuracy on MRPC compared to AUBER's 86.03%. This contradicts the claim that AUBER outperforms all competitors. The text should either qualify the claim to exclude the ablation variants or acknowledge that AUBER-Reverse performs better on MRPC.

comments.title: Missing 9.39% improvement calculation
comments.quote: While most of the competitors fail to improve performance of BERT on the dev dataset of MRPC and CoLA, AUBER improves the performance of BERT by up to  $9.39\%$  on those datasets.
comments.explanation: The paper claims AUBER improves performance by up to 9.39% on MRPC and CoLA, but Table 3 only shows AUBER's absolute accuracy scores (86.03% for MRPC, 61.28 for CoLA). Without the baseline BERT performance on these datasets, readers cannot verify the 9.39% improvement claim. The text should either provide the baseline BERT scores for comparison or remove the specific percentage claim if it cannot be substantiated from the presented data.

comments.title: Action space definition inconsistency
comments.quote: For a BERT model with $H$ attention heads per layer, the number of possible actions is $H+1$ (i.e. $a\in\{1,2,\ldots,H,H+1\}$). When the action $a=i\in\{1,2,\ldots,H-1,H\}$ is chosen, the corresponding $i^{th}$ attention head is pruned.
comments.explanation: The action space definition has an inconsistency between the stated range and the description. The text defines actions as $a\in\{1,2,\ldots,H,H+1\}$ but then describes pruning actions as $a=i\in\{1,2,\ldots,H-1,H\}$. This means action H+1 is the quit action, but the indexing creates ambiguity: are heads numbered 1 to H or 0 to H-1? Rewrite the action space as $a\in\{0,1,2,\ldots,H-1\}$ where actions 0 to H-1 prune the corresponding head, or clarify that heads are 1-indexed and actions are $a\in\{1,2,\ldots,H,H+1\}$ with H+1 as quit.

comments.title: Reward calculation baseline ambiguity
comments.quote:
$\Delta acc=current\_accuracy-prev\_accuracy,$ (4)

where $current\_accuracy$ is the accuracy of the current BERT model evaluated on the mini-dev set, and $prev\_accuracy$ is the accuracy obtained from the previous state or the accuracy of the original BERT model if no attention heads are pruned.
comments.explanation: The reward calculation has an ambiguity in how prev_accuracy is determined. The text says it's either "the accuracy obtained from the previous state or the accuracy of the original BERT model if no attention heads are pruned." This creates inconsistency: if multiple heads have been pruned, is prev_accuracy the accuracy after the last pruning action, or is it always compared to the original unpruned model? Specify that prev_accuracy is always the accuracy from the immediately preceding state to maintain consistent incremental reward signals.

comments.title: Undefined epsilon decay parameter
comments.quote: For the epsilon greedy strategy for choosing actions,  $\epsilon_{initial}$  and  $\epsilon_{final}$  are set to 1 and 0.05 respectively, and the epsilon decreases exponentially with the decay rate  $\epsilon_{decay}$  of 256.
comments.explanation: The paper states epsilon decreases exponentially with decay rate 256 but doesn't specify the exact formula. Without the exact decay equation, the parameter value of 256 is meaningless and the setup is not reproducible. The authors should specify the complete epsilon decay formula.

comments.title: Typo in state representation notation
comments.quote: Q2 State Representation (Section 4.3). How useful is the  $LI$  norm of the value matrices of attention heads in representing the state of BERT?
comments.explanation: The text contains a typo where "$LI$" should be "$L1$". Throughout the paper, the authors consistently refer to the L1 norm, so this appears to be a LaTeX rendering error where the numeral "1" was rendered as "I".

comments.title: Table 3 reference inconsistency
comments.quote: Table 3: We compare AUBER with four variants: AUBER-Query, AUBER-Key, AUBER-L2, and AUBER-Reverse on four GLUE datasets.
comments.explanation: The text states Table 3 compares AUBER with four variants, but the table actually shows five variants including AUBER itself. The caption should be corrected to "Table 3: We compare AUBER and four variants: AUBER-Query, AUBER-Key, AUBER-L2, and AUBER-Reverse on four GLUE datasets."

comments.title: Grammatical error in dropout sentence
comments.quote: Variants of dropout improves the stability of fine-tuning large pre-trained language models even when presented with a small number of training examples (*Lee et al. (2020); Fan et al. (2020)*).
comments.explanation: The subject "Variants of dropout" is plural, but the verb "improves" is singular. Rewrite "Variants of dropout improves the stability" as "Variants of dropout improve the stability" because the subject "variants" requires a plural verb.

comments.title: Inconsistent citation formatting
comments.quote: (*Voita et al. (2019); Michel et al. (2019); Kovaleva et al.; McCarley (2019)*)
comments.explanation: The citation format is inconsistent within the parenthetical reference. 'Kovaleva et al.' is missing the year, while all other citations include years. Rewrite as '(*Voita et al. (2019); Michel et al. (2019); Kovaleva et al. (2019); McCarley (2019)*)' assuming the correct year is 2019.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper presents AUBER, a reinforcement learning-based approach to prune attention heads in BERT for regularization. By utilizing a DQN agent to decide which heads to remove in a layer-wise fashion, the authors claim to improve performance on small-scale datasets compared to heuristic-based pruning.

The paper addresses a practical problem in fine-tuning BERT on limited data. While the use of RL to automate pruning is interesting, the experimental design and the theoretical justification for the state representation require more rigor to be convincing.

Overview issues:

- Weak Theoretical Justification for State Representation: Theorem 1 attempts to justify using the L1 norm of the value matrix as a state representation by bounding the L1 norm of the head's output. However, the bound || O_i ||_1 <= C || W_i^V ||_1 assumes that the attention weights are constant or bounded in a way that ignores the dynamic nature of the softmax output in Equation 2. Since the attention weights depend on the input Q and K, which change during fine-tuning, the constant C is not stationary. The paper should either provide an empirical analysis showing how this bound holds across different inputs or acknowledge that this is a heuristic proxy rather than a formal guarantee of importance.

- Confounding Factors in Fine-tuning: The methodology describes fine-tuning the model after each layer is pruned (Section 3.2). This creates a significant confounder: the observed accuracy improvements might stem from the additional fine-tuning steps rather than the specific pruning policy learned by the DQN agent. To isolate the contribution of the RL agent, the authors must compare AUBER against a baseline that performs the same number of fine-tuning iterations but uses a random or heuristic-based pruning order.

- Computational Overhead and Scalability: The paper claims to reduce the search space through dually-greedy pruning, yet the training of the DQN agent for 150 episodes per layer (Section 4.1) is computationally intensive. The paper lacks a discussion on the total wall-clock time required for this search process compared to static pruning methods. Given that the goal is regularization for small datasets, if the search time exceeds the time required to simply train a smaller model or use standard dropout, the practical utility of AUBER is limited.

- Lack of Pareto Frontier Analysis: The evaluation focuses on specific accuracy improvements but fails to report the Pareto frontier between model size and task performance. In Table 2, it is unclear if the competitors were allowed to prune the same number of heads as AUBER or if the comparison is biased by the specific pruning ratio chosen by the RL agent. A more robust evaluation would include a plot showing accuracy versus the percentage of pruned heads to demonstrate that AUBER consistently dominates other methods across different compression levels.

- Need for a concrete Pareto frontier analysis across pruning ratios: The current evaluation compares AUBER to baselines at a single, fixed pruning ratio for each task, which makes it difficult to assess if AUBER is truly superior or simply operating at a more favorable point on the performance-complexity trade-off. To demonstrate the robustness of the learned policy, the authors should produce a Pareto frontier plot for each GLUE task, showing validation accuracy on the y-axis and the number of pruned heads on the x-axis. This would clarify whether the 9.39% improvement is a consistent gain across the entire range of compression or if it only appears at specific, potentially arbitrary, pruning levels.

- Inference latency and hardware-aware throughput documentation: The paper claims to improve BERT performance via regularization through pruning, yet it neglects the practical implications of this pruning on inference speed. While the model size is reduced, the actual latency on target hardware like a GPU depends on the specific implementation of the sparse attention mechanism. The authors should include a measurement of the wall-clock inference time for the pruned models compared to the original BERT-base, as this is a standard requirement for any paper claiming to improve model efficiency or utility in downstream tasks.

- Ablation on the impact of the fine-tuning schedule: The methodology involves a complex process of layer-wise pruning followed by fine-tuning at each stage. It is unclear whether the performance gains are driven by the RL-discovered pruning policy or by the intensive, layer-wise fine-tuning schedule itself. To isolate the contribution of the RL agent, the authors should perform a control experiment where a random or heuristic-based pruning order is subjected to the exact same layer-wise fine-tuning regimen. Without this, the paper fails to prove that the 'automated' policy is the primary driver of the observed accuracy improvements.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper needs to better isolate the effects of its pruning policy from the repeated fine-tuning process and provide a more thorough comparison against baselines that account for the total computational budget. The theoretical claims regarding the state representation also require clarification to avoid overstating their formal significance.

Revision targets:

- Perform a controlled ablation that isolates the RL-learned pruning policy from the iterative fine-tuning process.

- Provide a Pareto frontier analysis (accuracy vs. number of heads) to demonstrate that the improvements are not just due to selecting a specific, perhaps coincidental, number of heads.

- Include a detailed runtime analysis comparing the search phase of AUBER to the training time of standard BERT fine-tuning.

- Clarify the limitations of Theorem 1 regarding the dependency of the bound on input-dependent attention weights.

comments.title: Ambiguity in performance improvement metric
comments.quote: Experimental results show that AUBER outperforms existing pruning methods by achieving up to $9.39\%$ better accuracy.
comments.explanation: The claim of a 9.39% improvement is ambiguous. In the context of GLUE benchmarks, accuracy gains are typically reported in absolute percentage points. If this figure represents a relative improvement, it should be explicitly stated as such to avoid misleading the reader. Please clarify whether this value is an absolute percentage point gain or a relative performance metric, and specify the baseline task where this gain was observed.

comments.title: Confounding factors in fine-tuning schedule
comments.quote: e best pruning policy for layer $l$ of BERT is found, the BERT model pruned according to the best pruning policy is fine-tuned with a smaller learning rate. This fine-tuning step is crucial since it adjusts the weights of remaining attention heads to compensate for the information lost due to pruning. Then, the
comments.explanation: The methodology involves intensive layer-wise fine-tuning after each pruning step. It is unclear whether the observed accuracy improvements are driven by the RL-discovered pruning policy or by the cumulative effect of this fine-tuning schedule. To isolate the contribution of the RL agent, please perform a control experiment comparing AUBER against a baseline that uses a random or heuristic-based pruning order but follows the exact same layer-wise fine-tuning regimen.

comments.title: Inconsistency in epsilon-greedy decay formula
comments.quote:
$\epsilon=\epsilon_{final}+\frac{\epsilon_{initial}-\epsilon_{final}}{e^{s/\epsilon_{decay}}}$ (3)

He
comments.explanation: The provided formula for epsilon decay is mathematically inconsistent with the stated behavior of converging to epsilon_final as s approaches infinity. With a positive exponent in the denominator, the term e^(s/epsilon_decay) grows, causing the fraction to approach zero, which is correct; however, this requires epsilon_decay to be negative to function as a decay constant. Please verify if the formula should be written as epsilon = epsilon_final + (epsilon_initial - epsilon_final) * exp(-s / epsilon_decay).

comments.title: Incomplete derivation of Theorem 1
comments.quote: n,  $\| O_i \|_1 \leq C \| W_i^V \|_1$  where the constant  $C = N \| V \|_1$ .
comments.explanation: The derivation of the bound ||O_i||_1 <= C ||W_i^V||_1 is incomplete because it ignores the internal structure of the attention mechanism. Given O_i = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)) V W_i^V, the bound should account for the properties of the attention matrix A = softmax(QK^T / sqrt(d_k)). Since A is row-stochastic, its induced L1 norm is 1. It is unclear how the constant C = N ||V||_1 is derived without accounting for the specific properties of A. Please rewrite the definition of C to explicitly include the bound on the attention matrix or clarify the norm definition being used.

comments.title: Ambiguity in terminal reward logic
comments.quote:
agent. The reward for action $a=H+1$ is a hyper-parameter that can be adjusted to encourage or discourage active pruning. In AUBER, it is set to $0$ to encourage the DQN agent to prune only when the expected change in accuracy is positive.

Fine-tuni
comments.explanation: Setting the terminal reward to 0 is potentially counterproductive. If the agent is encouraged to prune only when the change in accuracy is positive, the terminal action (quitting) should represent the value of the current state. By setting the terminal reward to 0, the agent is effectively told that stopping is always neutral, which may incentivize the agent to continue pruning even when marginal gains are negative, provided the cumulative reward remains higher than the zero-reward terminal state. Please clarify how this choice prevents over-pruning.

comments.title: Inconsistency in terminology for Q, K, V
comments.quote: t. We compute the query, key, and value vectors by multiplying the input embeddings  $Q, K, V \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$  with the parameterized ma
comments.explanation: The text refers to Q, K, and V as 'vectors' while explicitly defining them as matrices in R^{N x d}. While a matrix is a collection of vectors, referring to the entire set of embeddings for N tokens as a 'vector' is imprecise. Please replace 'vectors' with 'matrices' to align with the provided dimensions.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper proposes AUBER, an RL-based method to automate BERT attention head pruning for regularization. While the idea of using reinforcement learning to replace static heuristics is interesting, the implementation relies heavily on a static weight-norm proxy for the state representation. The experimental design contains unsupported claims regarding pruning budgets, the reward function is poorly matched to the DQN architecture, and critical efficiency metrics are missing. These issues require substantial methodological and experimental revisions.

The paper tackles a relevant problem in NLP model compression and regularization. The application of DQN to sequential head pruning shows creativity, and the ablation studies on state representations and layer ordering provide some empirical insight. However, the theoretical justification for the state vector overlooks fundamental attention mechanics, and the experimental validation does not align with the stated claims. The work needs stronger theoretical grounding and a more rigorous evaluation framework to establish its practical value.

Overview issues:

- State representation contradicts the core claim of avoiding heuristics: Section 3.3 defines the state solely as the L1 norm of the value matrix weights. The reinforcement learning agent merely learns a static ordering based on this proxy rather than discovering dynamic, task-specific importance. This reduces AUBER to a heuristic search algorithm wrapped in an RL framework. Readers will question whether the agent actually contributes to regularization beyond a simple weight-norm sort. The authors should explicitly acknowledge this limitation or enrich the state vector with dynamic features like attention entropy or gradient sensitivity to justify the RL component.

- Experimental evaluation does not support the stated performance claim: The abstract and Table 2 caption assert that AUBER achieves the best accuracy for the same number of pruned heads. Table 3 reveals that AUBER prunes 20 heads on MRPC, while AUBER-Reverse prunes 58 heads to reach a slightly higher accuracy. The method never enforces a fixed pruning budget across baselines. This mismatch invalidates the direct comparison and makes the same number of pruned heads claim false. The revision must either control the pruning ratio strictly during evaluation or remove the claim and present results at matched sparsity levels.

- Reward function is ill-suited for the DQN architecture: Section 3.2 defines the reward as the discrete change in validation accuracy. Accuracy acts as a step function, producing sparse and noisy signals that destabilize Q-value updates. The authors claim this delta provides a normalization effect, but it does not resolve the fundamental mismatch between discrete rewards and value-based learning. A more stable approach would use a continuous surrogate, such as the drop in log-likelihood or KL divergence between original and pruned models. Implementing a smoother reward signal would improve training stability and make the learned policy more reliable.

- Theoretical justification for the state representation overlooks attention mechanics: Theorem 1 bounds the output L1 norm by the value matrix weights, ignoring the softmax attention weights derived from queries and keys. Equation 2 shows that attention gates the value vectors, meaning a head with large V weights can still contribute nothing if attention weights are near zero. The bound therefore fails to capture how heads actually process information. The authors should either revise the theorem to incorporate attention weight bounds or provide empirical evidence showing that value norms reliably predict head importance despite this gating mechanism.

- Missing efficiency metrics and computational cost analysis: Pruning research typically demands reports on FLOPs, parameter reduction, and inference latency alongside accuracy. The authors focus exclusively on regularization gains without quantifying the computational savings. Furthermore, the RL training phase requires fine-tuning the model after every single head pruning decision, a process that scales poorly and remains unmeasured. Adding latency benchmarks and a clear breakdown of search time versus inference gains would establish the practical viability of the method.

- Baseline comparisons lack coverage of modern regularization and automated pruning techniques: The experiments only include older heuristic methods like Michel et al. and Voita et al., ignoring recent approaches like Mixout, SMART, or automated search strategies like A* pruning. This leaves a gap in understanding how AUBER stacks up against current state-of-the-art solutions. The authors should extend the evaluation to include these contemporary methods and test the approach on larger models or diverse tasks to demonstrate broader applicability.

- Worked example validating the L1 norm bound on attention output: Theorem 1 bounds the L1 norm of an attention head's output by its value matrix weights, but it does not demonstrate how this bound behaves in practice. Readers cannot calibrate their intuition about the theorem's tightness or relevance without a concrete computation. The authors should compute the L1 norm bound for a simple, fully-specified two-head attention model on a synthetic sequence and compare it to the actual accuracy drop when that head is removed. This calculation would show whether the bound is loose or informative, and whether it reliably identifies heads whose removal hurts performance. Without this worked example, the theoretical justification remains an abstract inequality disconnected from the pruning task.

- Missing discussion on RL search overhead and scalability: Algorithm 1 requires fine-tuning the model after every single head pruning decision, and the DQN agent trains for 150 episodes per layer. The paper never quantifies this search cost or discusses how it scales to larger models or datasets. A method claiming to automate regularization must address the practical feasibility of its search phase. The authors should provide a concrete breakdown of the RL training time versus the inference-time gains achieved by the pruned model. They should also discuss strategies to mitigate the computational burden, such as caching value norms across tasks or using a single policy network initialized across layers, to show the approach is viable beyond the reported experiments.

- Lack of analysis on policy transfer across tasks: The abstract and introduction frame AUBER as a method that learns a pruning policy to automate regularization, but the experimental setup trains a fresh DQN agent for each task. The paper does not investigate whether the learned policy generalizes to unseen tasks or if it must be retrained from scratch every time. This leaves a critical gap in understanding the method's practical utility. The authors should test whether a policy trained on one GLUE dataset can prune effectively on another without retraining, or explicitly acknowledge that the policy is task-specific. Developing this implication would clarify whether AUBER learns transferable structural priors or merely overfits to the reward signal of a single dataset.
